idea.h2: Authority comes from the device. Never the network.
idea.p: The phone decides — nothing else. A model, a script, an assistant on your laptop: each gets in only through a connection you opened, and touches only what you allowed.

cells.p: Control rides a USB cable or an adb-over-Wi-Fi session you started. No internet. No server. Your screen stays between the two machines.
An empty grant table can't touch a thing. No denylist to keep current — only what you switched on.
Every session is timed, pinned to an ongoing notification with a stop button, and logged action by action.
Empty the grants and it can't act. Rotate the key and it can't connect. Uninstall and it's gone.

backends.h2: Start with adb. Upgrade to the app.
backends.p: Same tools either way — the model can't tell them apart. The app just gives the phone more to say back.

panels.name: adb
panels.sub: No app. Any phone with USB debugging or adb-over-Wi-Fi.
items: A full shell, device-wide — exactly what USB debugging grants
panels.foot: no per-app grants · no clipboard · no notification list

panels.name: on-device app
panels.sub: Tunnels to the app's loopback server over an adb forward it opens itself.
items: Accessibility backend — semantic tree, real text entry
Timed sessions with an ongoing notification
panels.foot: sealed frames · loopback only · falls back to adb

layers.p: Root isn't always better — su -c input tap forks a process per gesture and trails the accessibility path. But root is the only screenshot with no capture dialog. The resolver picks per operation.
head: Operation
Preferred
Fallback

scopes.h2: Per package. Per scope. Revocable. Expiring by default.
scopes.p: Grants live in the app. The pure-adb path has none — adb is device-wide shell power, stated plainly, not dressed down.
head: Scope
Permits
rows: bring an app to the front
run shell commands, while the app is open

security.p: Loopback isn't a wall — any app on the phone can dial a local port. The pairing key decides who gets an answer.

cells.p: Both public keys go by QR, both directions. Nothing sits in the handshake. Keys stay encrypted on the device and never leave it.
Every message is sealed and authenticated. A frame that won't open gets no reply — not even a hint the device is there.
A random salt in the hello derives new keys per connection. Relaunch the client; no nonce ever repeats.
The channel binds to IPv4 loopback and dials nothing out. Reachable only through an adb-forward tunnel you set up.

setup.h2: Plugged in to acting, in four.

steps.html: USB debugging on and plugged in — or adb connect <ip>:5555 over the network. Check with adb devices.

steps.h: Point your host at the server
steps.html: Run mcpserved. Unpaired, it drives the phone straight over adb. Ready now.
Want accessibility, grants, and the notification mirror? Install the app, run mcpserved pair, and scan both ways.
Accept the disclosure, enable the service, arm it, and grant the packages you want reachable. The server upgrades to the app on its own.

notes.h: The screen stays on in an app session
notes.p: Lock the device and the node tree empties. Keep sessions short.
No root means a manual re-pair after every restart.
Spaces work; newlines and most non-ASCII don't. The app handles the rest.

notes.h: adb has no clipboard or notifications

notes.p: No third-party iOS app can automate another. Apple's rule, not ours.
The relay's gone, but accessibility use is declared — with a prominent in-app notice.

data.h2: Nothing collected. Nowhere to send it.
